section "Quasi-coherent sheaves" / "The structure sheaf", tags around 01HR–01I8).

49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.

71	# Reference Retriever Directive

75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>

83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.

102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**

106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.

146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."

150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.

153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. 185	- references/<slug>.tex — LaTeX source, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>   ← if obtained
186	(List every file you saved and the EXACT URL it came from. These successful links are
187	the most important field — a later planner uses them to re-fetch or double-check.)
188

191

204	```

206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).
209	
210	```markdown
211	# <Title>
212	
213	## Citation

215

218

221

223	- <url 1> — <what happened: 404 / paywall / HTML-only login page / corrupt file>
224	- <url 2> — <…>

227	## Why it matters / what to do next
228	<which chapter/decision needed this, and the dispatcher's options: find another route, pick a different source, or treat the dependent block as gated on retrieval>
229	
230	<!-- NO contents map, NO abstract, NO recalled statements. There is nothing verified to record. -->
231	```

233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

273

275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```

303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.

2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.

32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

580	\label{definition-standard-covering}
581	Let $R$ be a ring.
582	\begin{enumerate}
583	\item A {\it standard open covering} of $\Spec(R)$
584	is a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$,
585	where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in R$.
586	\item Suppose that $D(f) \subset \Spec(R)$ is a standard
587	open. A {\it standard open covering} of $D(f)$
588	is a covering $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$,
589	where $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$.
590	\end{enumerate}
591	\end{definition}
592	
593	\noindent
594	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. We will define
595	a presheaf $\widetilde M$ on the basis of standard opens.
596	Suppose that $U \subset \Spec(R)$ is a standard open.
597	If $f, g \in R$ are such that $D(f) = D(g)$, then
598	by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above there are canonical
599	maps $M_f \to M_g$ and $M_g \to M_f$ which are mutually inverse.
600	Hence we may choose any $f$ such that $U = D(f)$
601	and define
602	$$
603	\widetilde M(U) = M_f.
604	$$
605	Note that if $D(g) \subset D(f)$, then by
606	Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above we have
607	a canonical map
608	$$
609	\widetilde M(D(f)) = M_f \longrightarrow M_g = \widetilde M(D(g)).
610	$$
611	Clearly, this defines a presheaf of abelian groups on the basis
612	of standard opens. If $M = R$, then $\widetilde R$ is a presheaf
613	of rings on the basis of standard opens.
614	
615	\medskip\noindent
616	Let us compute the stalk of $\widetilde M$ at a point $x \in \Spec(R)$.
617	Suppose that $x$ corresponds to the prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$.
618	By definition of the stalk we see that
619	$$
620	\widetilde M_x = \colim_{f\in R, f\not\in \mathfrak p} M_f
621	$$
622	Here the set $\{f \in R, f \not \in \mathfrak p\}$ is preordered by
623	the rule $f \geq f' \Leftrightarrow D(f) \subset D(f')$.
624	If $f_1, f_2 \in R \setminus \mathfrak p$, then we have
625	$f_1f_2 \geq f_1$ in this ordering. Hence by
626	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-localization-colimit}
627	we conclude that
628	$$
629	\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}.
630	$$
631	
632	\medskip\noindent
633	Next, we check the sheaf condition for the standard open coverings.
634	If $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$, then the sheaf condition
635	for this covering is equivalent with the exactness of the
636	sequence
637	$$
638	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{g_i} \to \bigoplus M_{g_ig_j}.
639	$$
640	Note that $D(g_i) = D(fg_i)$, and hence we can rewrite this
641	sequence as the sequence
642	$$
643	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{fg_i} \to \bigoplus M_{fg_ig_j}.
644	$$

646	we see that $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal
647	in $R_f$. Thus we may apply
648	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}
649	to the module $M_f$ over $R_f$ and the elements $g_1, \ldots, g_n$.
650	We conclude that the sequence is exact. By the remarks
651	made above, we see that $\widetilde M$ is a sheaf
652	on the basis of standard opens.
653	
654	\medskip\noindent
655	Thus we conclude from the material in
656	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-bases}
657	that there exists a
658	unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
659	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the standard opens.

662	
663	\medskip\noindent
664	Similarly, for any $R$-module $M$ there exists

670	\begin{definition}
671	\label{definition-structure-sheaf}
672	Let $R$ be a ring.
673	\begin{enumerate}
674	\item The {\it structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ of the
675	spectrum of $R$} is the unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
676	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the basis of standard opens.
677	\item The locally ringed space
678	$(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$ is called
679	the {\it spectrum} of $R$ and denoted $\Spec(R)$.
680	\item The sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
681	extending $\widetilde M$ to all opens of $\Spec(R)$
682	is called the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
683	associated to $M$. This sheaf is denoted $\widetilde M$ as
684	well.
685	\end{enumerate}
686	\end{definition}
687	
688	\noindent
689	We summarize the results obtained so far.
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	\label{lemma-spec-sheaves}
693	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
694	be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
695	associated to $M$.
696	\begin{enumerate}
697	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R$.
698	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \widetilde M) = M$ as an $R$-module.
699	\item For every $f \in R$ we have
700	$\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R_f$.
701	\item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
702	as an $R_f$-module.
703	\item Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
704	on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
705	are the maps
706	$R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
707	\ref{lemma-standard-open}.
708	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
709	be the corresponding point. We have
710	$\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R), x} = R_{\mathfrak p}$.
711	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
712	be the corresponding point. We have $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$
713	as an $R_{\mathfrak p}$-module.
714	\end{enumerate}
715	Moreover, all these identifications are functorial in the $R$
716	module $M$. In particular, the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
717	is an exact functor from the category of $R$-modules
718	to the category of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules.
719	\end{lemma}
720	
721	\begin{proof}
722	Assertions (1) - (7) are clear from the discussion above.

# Lemma 26.7.1 Summary

**Tag Number:** 01I7

**Lemma Label:** Lemma 26.7.1

**Key Statements about $\widetilde{M}$:**

The lemma establishes that for an affine scheme and an R-module M, there exists "a canonical isomorphism between the sheaf $\widetilde{M}$ associated to the R-module M" and another naturally defined sheaf.

The principal findings are:

1. **Quasi-coherence:** The sheaves $\widetilde{M}$ are quasi-coherent sheaves on the affine scheme.

2. **Mapping Property:** They satisfy a fundamental universal property: "the sheaves $\widetilde{M}$ are characterized by the following mapping property" expressing that morphisms from $\widetilde{M}$ to any sheaf of modules correspond to module homomorphisms from M to global sections.

3. **Functoriality:** The isomorphism is functorial in M, meaning it respects the underlying algebraic structure.

# Lemma 26.5.4

**Label:** 01HV

**Key statements about $\widetilde{M}$ on $D(f)$:**

The lemma establishes that for an $R$-module $M$ and its associated sheaf $\widetilde{M}$:

1. "For every $f \in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde{M}) = M_f$ as an $R_f$-module."

2. The restriction mappings correspond to the canonical maps $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma 26.5.1 when $D(g) \subset D(f)$.

3. At any point $x \in \text{Spec}(R)$ corresponding to prime $\mathfrak{p}$, the stalk is "$\widetilde{M}_x = M_{\mathfrak{p}}$ as an $R_{\mathfrak{p}}$-module."

Additionally, the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde{M}$ is exact, preserving short exact sequences of $R$-modules.
